evidence_quotes.quote: remotely, and at their discretion, remove a property from a customer.
permanent license that does not include an "expiry date" so to speak.

paragraphs.text: Hello, I would like to suggest an extension of the protection offered by the discussed act. At present it does not protect the ownership and usage of server dependent software such as video games, which by all definition should now be also considered works of culture, especially considering the amount of diversity in both teams and assets they require to be functional. Many such works have been rendered irrecoverable due to being dependent on a continuous maintenance of external (publisher supplied) network infrastructure, even when such product was sold on a permanent license that does not include an "expiry date" so to speak. It is both a loss of cultural heritage and in my opinion a legally grey area that allows a seller to remotely, and at their discretion, remove a property from a customer. Regardless of the initial selling conditions or license agreements. I believe it is in the best interest of both the parties responsible for these works of art, and the general public, that these should be possible to be archived, in the same way as works of literature, music, cinema and any other medium are. Thank you for your consideration and time.
